title: Chapter 8: The Archive
content: Chloe had been inside Serenity for ten days. In that time, she'd mapped the building, cracked a keypad, stolen two tablets, built a network, recruited three allies, and sent a flare to the outside world that was now, somewhere beyond the redwoods, turning into a story.
But walking onto the second floor felt different.

It wasn't the architecture — same beige walls, same lavender-scented air, same cameras blinking their red heartbeats. It was the people. The women on the second floor moved differently than the ones on the first. They walked slower. Talked softer. Their eyes had a quality that Chloe recognized but couldn't name until she saw it on her own face in the reflection of a hallway window.
They weren't broken. They were patients who'd learned, over years, to look broken.

Rachel Okonkwo was in the corner of the common room, exactly where Maya had described. Same chair. Same copy of The New England Journal of Medicine. Same stillness — the kind of stillness that took years to perfect.
Chloe sat down across from her. "I'm Chloe."

Rachel studied her for a long moment. Not like Holloway studied her — as a problem to be solved. Like a peer. Like someone who'd been studying institutional power for decades and was evaluating whether the person in front of her actually understood the game.
"Maya says you want to burn this place down," Rachel said.
"Metaphorically."
"That's what everyone says." Rachel turned a page of her journal. "Then they get tired. Or transferred. Or medicated into compliance. Burn turns into simmer turns into acceptance."

"I've been here ten days," Chloe said. "During which I've stolen two tablets, hacked your internal network, and sent an email to a journalist at The Verge who is currently investigating this facility. I haven't had time to get tired."
Chloe's stomach went cold. Cold in a way that had nothing to do with fear.
"How many?"

"Seventeen current patients. Twenty-three former ones." Rachel flipped through the pages. "Sexual assault cover-ups. Whistleblower retaliation. A woman committed because she converted to a different religion than her parents. A woman committed because she fell in love with someone her family didn't approve of. A woman committed because she inherited money her brothers wanted."
"This isn't a hospital," Chloe said.
"No." Rachel's eyes met hers. "It's a disposal system. For inconvenient women."
The words hung in the air like smoke.

Ten minutes later, Chloe was back on the first floor, the weight of Rachel's files pressing against her chest even though she wasn't carrying them. Rachel had kept the physical pages — too dangerous to move — but Chloe had memorized the key ones. The ones that mattered.
She found Layla in the laundry room, tablet in her lap, fingers still even though her mind was clearly racing.
"I met Rachel," Chloe said.
"And?"
"She's got the intake files. The real ones. Every patient. Every reason. Every family that paid Sterling to make their daughter disappear."
Layla's hand went to her mouth. "That's... that's enough to shut this place down."
"It's enough to send people to prison."
The plan was simple, which was why it worked.

Sophia, whose eight months of staff surveillance had taught her every schedule in the building, knew that the cafeteria workstation — the old Linux box Layla had identified — was still online. The network audit had locked down patient access, but the cafeteria terminal was on a separate subnet. Kitchen inventory. Not patient-facing. Not flagged in the security sweep.
"Kevin from IT doesn't know it exists," Sophia said. "It's been running since 2019 on an IP address that isn't even in the current network map."
"So we use it as a decoy," Chloe said. "Layla, can you push a Wi-Fi signal from that terminal?"

"I can't push Wi-Fi from a wired terminal. But I can enable its Bluetooth adapter. The spectrum analyzer will pick up Bluetooth transmissions just as easily as Wi-Fi. He'll think there's a rogue device in the cafeteria."
"And while he's searching the cafeteria..."
"We keep the tablets in the laundry room. Powered off. Chips intact. He'll walk right past us."
Maya crossed her arms. "This is a delaying action."
"Everything in here is a delaying action," Chloe said. "The question is whether we can delay long enough for Anna Delgado to get here."
That night, the new security guard — the one with the military posture — did something none of them expected.

She stopped at Chloe's door. Not for a room check. Not for a med count. She stopped, looked directly at the camera in the corner of the ceiling, and then — so fast Chloe almost missed it — she winked.
Chloe lay perfectly still.
Had she imagined it? A trick of the red camera light? The guard's face was unreadable, the same stone mask she'd worn since replacing Rodriguez.
But then the guard's hand moved — a flicker, a fraction of a second — and a folded piece of paper slid under Chloe's door.
Chloe waited until the footsteps faded. Then she unfolded the paper.
Two words, in handwriting so sharp it looked like it had been carved:
*I KNOW.*
